nativeRange.habitat: Coral-rich reefs and rocky areas in clear, well-lit shallow water.
nativeRange.biotope: Red Sea / Arabian Sea coral reef

diet.notes: Primarily grazes filamentous and macroalgae in the wild. In captivity, offer frequent feedings of marine-based seaweed (nori sheets clipped in the tank), spirulina, and vegetable-based prepared foods at least 2-3 times daily. A vitamin-rich, algae-heavy diet helps reduce aggression and supports immunity, with HLLE risk rising on poor diets.

goodWith: most peaceful reef fish of different body shape
avoidWith: small timid fish that can be bullied

summary: A striking Red Sea endemic surgeonfish with deep purple body and a bright yellow tail. Generally considered aggressive for a Zebrasoma; best kept singly in a large reef tank with ample open swimming space.
careNotes: The Purple Tang is a Red Sea endemic that also occurs in the Arabian Sea, Gulf of Aden, Persian Gulf, and Maldives, where it grazes filamentous algae over coral and rocky reefs at 0-20 m. Adults reach roughly 20-25 cm in captivity (FishBase reports up to ~37 cm TL in the wild), and have been reported to live 10+ years in well-kept reef systems. Tanks of around 500 L (130+ US gal) with a long footprint are generally advised to give them the open swimming space and grazing surface they require. Like all surgeonfishes, they possess a sharp, scalpel-like spine on each side of the caudal peduncle that can injure handlers and tankmates - take care when netting. Purple Tangs are noticeably more aggressive than most other Zebrasoma; conspecifics and other tangs (especially same-genus or similarly shaped fish) should generally be avoided unless the tank is very large and the tangs are added simultaneously. They are reef-safe and rarely bother corals or invertebrates. As high-metabolism grazers, they should have continuous access to algae (clipped nori, dried seaweed, macroalgae) and tend to decline rapidly without it; insufficient vitamin- and algae-rich diet plus poor water quality predispose them to Head and Lateral Line Erosion (HLLE). Cross-source notes: LiveAquaria states 72-78°F (22-26°C) while FishBase reports a wild range of 24-28°C; we averaged to 23-27°C. LiveAquaria suggests a 130 US gal (~492 L) minimum; we rounded to 500 L per the project's allowed tank-size set.
breedingNotes: Not commercially bred in captivity. Spawning occurs in the wild as pelagic broadcast events; rearing the planktonic larvae through metamorphosis has not been reliably achieved in the home aquarium.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Product care sheet: 130 gal min, 72-78F, pH 8.1-8.4, SG 1.020-1.025, dKH 8-12, semi-aggressive (very aggressive toward other Zebrasoma), reef safe, herbivore, ~10 in adult.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Acanthuridae, Acanthuriformes), max length 36.7 cm TL, depth 0-20 m, temperature 24-28C, distribution Red Sea to Persian Gulf with Maldives records, IUCN Least Concern.

additional.name: Wikipedia
additional.notes: Cross-checked native range (Red Sea, Gulf of Aden, Arabian Peninsula, Persian Gulf, Socotra), juvenile color phase distinct from adults, common name 'Yellowtail Tang'.
